title: Add real-time presence to chat with Socket.IO while keeping v1 polling API stable
business_context: Mobile clients on v1 use HTTP polling every 3s for new messages, generating 40% of total backend load. Web users complain about 3s message latency. We need real-time delivery for web while keeping mobile v1 polling working until mobile teams ship their v2 in 6 months.
constraints: Cannot break v1 polling endpoints used by 200k mobile users
Must support 50k concurrent socket connections
Presence updates must not exceed 100 messages/sec per room
JWT auth must work identically for HTTP and sockets
Server must run behind a load balancer with multiple instances

before_state.description: Express controllers handle message fetch via polling. No real-time layer. Single-instance memory state.

issues: No real-time push - clients must poll
No presence concept at all
Service tightly couples persistence to delivery
Cannot scale beyond one node since no shared pub/sub
Side effects (create) do not emit events - no hook for broadcasting
No way to track who is online
3s polling latency
No presence indicator
Duplicate fetch on re-render due to lastTime in deps causes infinite refetch loop on slow networks

reasoning: The naive approach is to rip out polling and replace with sockets. That breaks mobile v1. Correct approach: introduce Socket.IO as a second delivery channel that mirrors what polling provides. The persistence layer is the source of truth - both delivery channels read from it. Step 1: Refactor the service so createMessage emits a domain event (EventEmitter or a typed bus). Controller still writes to DB; bus dispatches to whichever listeners exist. Step 2: Add a Socket.IO namespace /chat with rooms per roomId. On socket connect, authenticate with the same JWT validator middleware exposed as a function. On message create event, broadcast to room. Step 3: Polling endpoint remains untouched - it queries DB. So mobile keeps working. Step 4: For multi-instance scaling, use the Redis adapter for Socket.IO so broadcasts cross nodes. Step 5: Presence is room membership - track via socket join/leave and a Redis Set per room with TTL refresh via heartbeat. NOT in-memory because that breaks across nodes. Step 6: Frontend gets a useSocket hook with proper cleanup. Replace setInterval polling with socket subscription. Keep polling code path behind a feature flag for fallback. Tradeoff considered: server-sent events are simpler but no bidirectional - needed for presence heartbeat and typing indicators. Rejected. Tradeoff: write directly from socket handler instead of HTTP - rejected because authorization, validation, rate limiting, audit logging all live in the controller layer and we don't want two write paths. Sockets only consume the bus; clients POST via HTTP.

after_state.description: Persistence layer emits domain events. Socket.IO namespace subscribes to the bus and fans out to rooms. Redis adapter enables horizontal scaling. Presence tracked in Redis with TTL. Frontend uses socket subscription with polling fallback.

files.purpose: Typed in-process event bus decoupling persistence from delivery channels
Service writes to DB then emits event - delivery channels are listeners, not callers
Socket.IO /chat namespace with JWT auth, Redis adapter for multi-instance, presence tracking, and bus subscription
React hook encapsulating socket lifecycle with proper cleanup and reconnection

migration_strategy: Step 1: Deploy the typed bus and service refactor with no behavioral change - polling endpoints still work
Step 2: Deploy Socket.IO namespace behind feature flag SOCKETS_ENABLED=false; verify infrastructure (Redis adapter, sticky sessions on LB)
Step 3: Enable sockets for 5% of web users via feature flag; monitor connection count, memory, Redis ops/sec
Step 4: Ramp to 100% web while polling stays on for mobile
Step 5: After 6 months when mobile v2 ships, mark v1 polling endpoints deprecated with Sunset header; remove after 90 days
testing_strategy: Integration tests spin up two server instances with shared Redis to verify cross-node broadcast. Load test with k6 simulating 10k concurrent sockets joining rooms. Contract tests pin v1 polling response shape. Chaos test: kill one instance mid-conversation, verify clients reconnect to other instance and resume receiving messages. Frontend: mock Socket.IO server in vitest to test useChatSocket cleanup on unmount.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would emit Socket.IO events directly from the controller after MessageModel.create. This works for one instance but breaks horizontally - the event only reaches sockets on the same node. They would also forget the Redis adapter, forget sticky sessions on the LB causing handshake failures, and tie presence to in-memory Maps that lose state on deploys.

title: Migrate anemic Order Mongoose model to DDD aggregate root with adapter for existing controllers
business_context: The Order domain has 23 invariants (cannot ship before paid, cannot refund more than charged, line items must sum to total, etc.) scattered across 8 controllers and 4 services. Bugs from violated invariants cause 12 customer service tickets per week. Need a single source of truth for Order rules without rewriting every consumer at once.
constraints: Cannot rewrite all 8 controllers in one PR
Existing API contracts must remain identical
Database schema changes must be backward compatible
Must support feature-flag rollback per controller

before_state.description: Anemic Order model - just a schema. Business rules live in services and controllers, often duplicated.

issues: No validation of invariants - total can be wrong, status transitions are not enforced
Schema is the model - any code can mutate fields without going through business logic
Business rules inline in controller - duplicated in 3 other places with subtle drift
Direct mutation of model fields bypasses any future validation
No domain event emitted for refund processing webhook

reasoning: DDD migration is risky if done all at once. Strategy: introduce Order aggregate as a pure TypeScript class in src/domain/order/. The aggregate owns invariants and emits domain events. It does NOT know about Mongoose. Build an OrderRepository in DAL that hydrates the aggregate from a Mongoose doc and persists it back. Existing controllers keep working unchanged. New code uses the aggregate. Migrate one controller at a time. Key insight: the aggregate must be reconstructable from a plain DTO (rehydration) and serializable back (snapshot). Mongoose document becomes a persistence detail. Tradeoff: full rewrite would be cleaner but blocks other work for months. Rejected. Tradeoff: use a library like Effect or Mikro-ORM - rejected due to introducing new framework debt. Plain classes are sufficient.

after_state.description: Order aggregate owns invariants. Repository pattern persists to Mongoose. Controllers transition one-by-one to use the aggregate via the repository.

files.purpose: Pure domain aggregate - owns invariants, emits events, no persistence knowledge
Repository hydrates aggregate from Mongoose and persists back, dispatching collected domain events on save
Controller becomes thin - calls aggregate method and lets domain enforce rules

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add aggregate + repository alongside existing code - no behavior change
Step 2: Convert refundOrder controller; deploy behind feature flag USE_AGGREGATE_ORDER_REFUND
Step 3: A/B test 10% traffic for one week - compare error rates, latency, business outcomes
Step 4: Ramp to 100%; convert next controller (placeOrder); repeat
Step 5: After all 8 controllers migrated, delete duplicated business logic and remove feature flags
Step 6: Make OrderModel internal to dal/ folder via lint rule preventing imports from outside
testing_strategy: Unit tests on OrderAggregate cover all invariants in isolation (no DB). Repository integration tests verify hydration/snapshot round-trip preserves all fields. Contract tests on the controller endpoint verify response shape unchanged. Snapshot diff on response bodies between old and new code paths during shadow traffic phase.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would put aggregate logic inside the Mongoose model as schema methods. This couples domain to persistence, makes the aggregate untestable without a Mongo connection, and makes hydration ambiguous (when is a doc valid? what about partial loads via projection?). They'd also forget that toSnapshot must round-trip identically or persistence drifts.

title: Replace 7-collection join dashboard endpoint with change-stream-fed read model
business_context: Admin dashboard endpoint /api/admin/overview takes 4.2s p99 and 1.8s p50, causing dashboard timeouts. It joins users, orders, payments, refunds, sessions, support_tickets, and audit_logs to compute KPIs. Adds 60% CPU load on primary Mongo. Admin team needs sub-300ms loads to triage incidents.
constraints: Cannot stop receiving writes during migration
Eventual consistency up to 2s is acceptable for KPIs
Existing endpoint response shape must not change
Must work with current Mongo 7.0 replica set without sharding

before_state.description: Single endpoint runs 7 aggregation pipelines in parallel and merges results in Node.

issues: Seven full collection scans on every request
No caching - every dashboard refresh hits primary
Contention with write traffic
Some aggregations require COLLSCAN on missing indexes

reasoning: Read model pattern: maintain a precomputed AdminOverview document updated by change streams from the 7 source collections. Endpoint becomes a single-document lookup. Tradeoff vs caching: simple Redis cache with TTL has thundering herd and shows stale data during incidents - exactly when freshness matters. Change streams give push-based updates. Tradeoff vs materialized view: Mongo's $merge views require periodic refresh - same staleness problem. Tradeoff vs separate analytics DB: overkill for one endpoint, adds infra. Implementation: a background worker connects to change streams for each collection, updates fields in AdminOverview doc atomically. Use $inc for counters where possible to avoid recomputation. Endpoint reads the single doc. Recovery: on worker startup, do a full recompute to seed the doc. Use resume tokens persisted to a separate collection for crash recovery without missing events.

after_state.description: Change-stream worker maintains AdminOverview document. Endpoint returns it directly. Initial seed on startup ensures correctness.

files.purpose: Single-document read model for dashboard KPIs
Change-stream consumer maintains read model with resume-token recovery
Single-doc lookup, response shape preserved with added _staleness for client diagnostics
Frontend surfaces staleness banner when read model lags

migration_strategy: Step 1: Deploy AdminOverview model and projector worker; let it build up data for 24h alongside existing endpoint
Step 2: Add shadow comparison endpoint that returns both old and new values with diff field; log diffs > 1%
Step 3: Fix any drift bugs in projector based on shadow comparison
Step 4: Switch endpoint to read model behind feature flag; old query runs every 5min as drift check
Step 5: Remove old aggregation code; keep drift check job running for 30 days
Step 6: Document recovery procedure: if drift > 5%, run full reseed script and pause projector
testing_strategy: Projector unit tests with mocked change streams verify each event type updates correctly. Integration test: spin up Mongo replica set in Docker, write a known sequence of orders/payments, assert read model matches. Drift detection: run old aggregation in parallel for a week, alert on any divergence > 0.1%. Chaos test: kill projector mid-stream, restart, verify it resumes via token without missing or duplicating events.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would add a Redis cache with 30s TTL in front of the existing aggregation. This helps p50 but doesn't help under load (thundering herd on cache miss), shows incorrect data during incidents, and still hammers Mongo when cache expires. They might also try to watch all 7 collections without resume tokens, losing events on every restart and silently drifting from truth.

title: Patch mass-assignment vulnerabilities across user, profile, and settings controllers with Zod whitelisting
business_context: Security audit found that PATCH /api/users/:id, PATCH /api/profile, and PATCH /api/settings all spread req.body directly into Mongoose updates. A penetration tester escalated a regular user to admin by passing {role:'admin'} in a profile update. The same vector works on three endpoints. We need a coherent fix that survives future endpoints being added.
constraints: Must not break existing legitimate clients sending extra fields
Must work in TypeScript strict mode
Existing API responses must not change
Audit log must record every successful change with before/after

before_state.description: Controllers spread req.body into update operations. No field-level allowlist.

issues: req.body is spread without filtering - {role:'admin'} is accepted
No authorization check that the requester can modify this user
No audit trail
Mass assignment - {verified:true, badges:['staff']} accepted
No size limits on free-text fields - DoS via huge bio strings
No client-side length limits matching what server should enforce
No error display - validation failures from new server return silently

reasoning: The fix is not just adding Zod to one controller - it's a systemic policy. Build a validateBody middleware factory that takes a Zod schema and parses req.body, replacing it with the parsed result. Schemas live next to controllers and explicitly list allowed fields. Any field not in the schema is stripped via .strict() with passthrough disabled. Add a separate authorization layer: who is allowed to change role? Only admins via a different endpoint. Tradeoff: class-validator gives decorators but adds reflect-metadata runtime overhead. Zod is plain TS, no runtime decoration, better inference. Tradeoff: pick one schema per HTTP method or use discriminated unions for role-based field allowlists. Discriminated unions chosen so admins can update more fields via the same endpoint with the same parsed type. For frontend, derive form types from shared Zod schemas in a /shared package so client and server cannot drift. Audit log via a wrapper that diffs before/after and writes to AuditLogModel.

after_state.description: Zod schemas define allowed fields per endpoint per role. Middleware enforces. Frontend derives types from shared schemas. Audit log captures all changes.

files.purpose: Single source of truth for user-update field allowlists - shared between client and server
Generic Zod validation middleware that overwrites req.body with parsed result, stripping unknown fields
Authorization, validated body (guaranteed by middleware), audit trail
Route picks schema by role so admins can update more fields via the same endpoint
Form uses the shared schema for client-side validation matching server exactly

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add shared schemas + validateBody middleware without applying to routes
Step 2: Apply validateBody to one endpoint, ship - monitor 4xx rate; legitimate clients sending extra fields will see 400s
Step 3: For two-week grace period, enable a LENIENT_MODE flag that logs would-fail validations as warnings instead of rejecting
Step 4: Notify API consumers of unknown fields being deprecated; provide migration guide
Step 5: Flip LENIENT_MODE off endpoint-by-endpoint
Step 6: Add lint rule forbidding direct req.body access outside of middleware files
testing_strategy: Security regression tests assert that PATCH with role/suspended/badges field by non-admin returns 400 and never modifies the field. Property-based test with fast-check generates arbitrary objects and asserts no field outside the schema persists. Frontend type tests verify form type equals schema inferred type. Penetration test repeats original exploit attempts.
what_whatbreaks_naive_approach: A junior would add manual delete req.body.role lines in each controller. This is fragile - new sensitive fields added later won't be deleted, and each controller drifts. They might also use mongoose schema strict:true alone, but Mongoose strict mode only protects fields not in the schema - role IS in the schema so it still gets through.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would add manual delete req.body.role lines in each controller. This is fragile - new sensitive fields added later won't be deleted, and each controller drifts. They might also rely on Mongoose strict:true, but that only protects fields not in the schema - role IS in the schema so it still gets through.

title: Consolidate four desyncing Cart contexts into single useSyncExternalStore-backed store with split read contexts
business_context: Cart bug reports peaked at 47/week: items appearing twice, totals not matching line items, badge in header out of sync with cart drawer. Root cause: cart state lives in CartItemsContext, CartTotalContext, CartBadgeContext, and a localStorage layer in CartPersistenceContext. Updates fan out via four useEffect chains that occasionally race. Need a single source of truth with no perceptible re-render regressions.
constraints: Cannot change public API of components consuming cart hooks
Must persist to localStorage across reloads
Must not cause re-render storms on item-count-only consumers
Must work with React 19 strict mode double-mount

before_state.description: Four contexts each holding a slice of cart state, with useEffects copying state between them.

issues: add allows duplicate productIds - no merge logic
No source-of-truth for derived total
Re-renders every consumer on any item change
Derived state stored in state - causes extra render
One render lag between items change and total update - badge briefly wrong
Same anti-pattern - derived state lagging items state
Provider order dependency - if mounted wrong, undefined behavior

reasoning: The bug is structural: derived state in state always lags source state by one render. The solution is a single external store. Use useSyncExternalStore from React 18+ for tear-free reads. Split contexts remain for ergonomics - one provider exposes the store, hooks read different slices via getSnapshot selectors. Selectors use Object.is comparison so component re-renders only when its slice changes. Total and badge become pure derivations of items, computed inside selectors. Tradeoff vs Zustand: external dep with similar mechanism; choosing useSyncExternalStore directly keeps it 30 lines and zero deps - but if the team prefers Zustand for devtools support, it's a 10-min swap. Tradeoff vs useReducer in single context: re-renders every consumer on any change - same problem we have. Persistence: subscribe to store changes, debounce write to localStorage. Hydration must happen synchronously in store factory to avoid SSR/strict-mode double-init issues. React 19 strict mode double-mounts effects - so store must be created outside component to keep identity stable.

after_state.description: Single store module owns cart state. Hooks use useSyncExternalStore with memoized selectors. Persistence is a subscriber. All four old providers consolidated to one.

files.purpose: Module-scoped external store with merge logic, persistence, and stable subscribe semantics
Selector-based hooks - components subscribe only to slices they read, re-render only when slice changes
Provider becomes a no-op wrapper - kept so existing app tree composition doesn't change
Badge consumer - re-renders only when count actually changes, not when prices or other fields change

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add new store module alongside old contexts; build hooks with same names but in a new file
Step 2: Migrate one consumer (CartBadge) to new hook; verify no visual regression in isolation
Step 3: Migrate consumers leaf-up: badge, drawer line items, totals view
Step 4: Replace CartItemsProvider with no-op CartProvider; old contexts now unused
Step 5: Run for one week; if no regressions, delete the four old context files
Step 6: Add ESLint rule banning new createContext for cart state
testing_strategy: Render test: mount Cart with 5 items, assert badge count, total, and items match a known sequence of actions. Re-render counter test using a render-count hook on each consumer - assert badge component re-renders 0 times when only unitPrice changes on an unrelated item. Persistence test: dispatch actions, reload page, assert state restored. Strict mode test: wrap with StrictMode and assert no duplicate adds.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would put everything in one useReducer inside a single context. Every consumer re-renders on every dispatch, so the badge re-renders when bio text changes. They'd also create the store inside the provider component using useState, which under React 19 strict mode double-mounts and resets state, losing the cart on every reload during dev.

title: Add collaborative cursors to document editor with debounced broadcast, smooth interpolation, and presence cleanup
business_context: Product wants Google-Docs-style collaborative cursors on the existing single-user document editor. Goal is to make collaboration feel alive without the 'jumping cursor' jank competitors have. Need 60fps cursor rendering for up to 20 simultaneous editors per document with low backend cost.
constraints: Cursor updates must not exceed 20 events/sec/user backend rate
Latency from user move to peers seeing it must be < 150ms p95
Stale cursors must disappear within 5s of user leaving or going idle
Must use existing Socket.IO infrastructure
Must not block keystrokes on the local user's editor

before_state.description: Document editor is single-user. Socket.IO exists for chat but no document namespace.

issues: No socket integration
No way to surface remote cursors
No presence info
No document namespace yet

reasoning: Two performance concerns dominate: backend throughput and frontend smoothness. Backend: each user emits cursor moves potentially every mousemove (~60Hz). With 20 users that's 1200 events/sec/doc. Solution: client-side throttle to 20Hz, server validates throttle via token bucket, server fans out via room broadcast (already-batched by Socket.IO). Frontend smoothness: incoming positions arrive at 20Hz but we want 60fps render. Solution: store target position per peer; in requestAnimationFrame loop interpolate from current to target with lerp factor 0.2. This hides network jitter and produces smooth motion. Idle detection: track last activity time per peer; if no event for 3s, fade cursor out; if 5s, remove. Use socket disconnect for hard remove. Selection tracking: in addition to position, broadcast selection range so peers see highlights too. Tradeoff vs WebRTC data channels: lower latency peer-to-peer but adds signaling, NAT traversal, and a fifth thing that can break. Reject. Tradeoff vs CRDT: overkill - cursor positions are last-writer-wins; no merge semantics needed.

after_state.description: Document namespace with rooms per docId. Client throttles, interpolates smoothly via rAF, fades idle cursors. Server enforces rate limit.

files.purpose: Document namespace with deterministic colors, rate limiting via token bucket, room-broadcast cursor positions
Hook combines socket lifecycle, client-side throttle, rAF-driven interpolation, idle fade-out
Editor reports cursor on every action and overlays interpolated remote cursors

migration_strategy: Step 1: Ship document namespace with no clients connected; verify no impact
Step 2: Add hook + cursor overlay behind feature flag COLLAB_CURSORS=true for staff only
Step 3: Validate metrics: events/sec/doc, p95 latency, FPS in dev tools
Step 4: Ramp to 10% then 100% for paying customers
Step 5: After stable for 30 days, remove feature flag
testing_strategy: Load test with 20 simulated users emitting moves at 60Hz - assert backend caps at 20Hz/user via token bucket. Visual test in Playwright: open two browsers, move cursor in one, screenshot the other to verify cursor shows up colored and positioned correctly. Idle test: move once, wait 6s, assert cursor removed from peer's view. Reconnect test: kill socket, restart, assert peer:join fires and other users see this user again.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would emit on every mousemove (60+Hz) with no throttle, saturating the backend. They'd render cursors via React state per cursor update, causing the textarea to lose focus on each re-render because React reconciles aggressively. They'd also forget that local-user cursor moves should NOT round-trip through the server - that adds latency to your own typing.

title: Build real-time 3D IoT fleet view with instanced meshes, LOD, and change-stream-driven updates
business_context: Operations team monitors 5,000 sensors across 12 warehouses. Current view is a flat list that auto-refreshes every 30s. Engineering wants a 3D warehouse view showing each sensor as a colored sphere with live temperature/humidity, so ops can spot regional issues at a glance. Must run smoothly on mid-range hardware (Intel Iris GPU).
constraints: Must maintain 60fps with 5,000 sensors visible
Updates from sensors arrive at up to 100 updates/sec total
No memory leaks - users keep this view open for entire shifts
Must work in latest Chrome and Firefox
Backend must not push more than 10 updates/sec/client

before_state.description: Flat table view fetched via REST every 30s. No 3D, no real-time.

issues: No real-time channel
No spatial coordinates - sensors only have warehouseId, not position
30s staleness too high for incident response
Doesn't scale visually to 5k rows

reasoning: Two parallel problems: getting updates from Mongo to clients in real-time, and rendering 5k objects at 60fps in WebGL. Backend: change stream on sensor reading collection → coalesce updates per sensor (latest-wins) in a 1s window → broadcast batched updates via Socket.IO to subscribed clients. Per-client backpressure: if client falls behind, drop frames. Frontend: 5k meshes = 5k draw calls = death. Use THREE.InstancedMesh with one geometry and 5k instance matrices. Color via instance color attribute. Update only the matrices/colors that changed in onUpdate handler. LOD: instances far from camera don't need updates - skip them. Disposal: scene cleanup on unmount must dispose geometry, material, instancedMesh, and remove change-stream subscription server-side via socket disconnect. Tradeoff vs sprites: cheaper but less convincing in 3D space. Tradeoff vs PointsMaterial: cheapest but loses color/size flexibility. InstancedMesh is the sweet spot. Backend coalescing matters because raw 100/sec broadcast to 50 clients = 5000 emit ops/sec; coalescing to 1Hz batches = 50 emits/sec.

after_state.description: Change-stream coalescer batches sensor updates, broadcasts at 1Hz. Frontend uses InstancedMesh with selective updates, LOD, and proper cleanup.

files.purpose: Change-stream consumer coalesces per-sensor updates into 1Hz batched broadcasts
Top-level r3f canvas with lighting and camera
InstancedMesh of 5k spheres - position once, recolor on socket batch, dispose on unmount
One-shot fetch of static sensor positions - positions rarely change so no live update needed

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add x/y/z fields to sensor schema; backfill positions from warehouse layout data
Step 2: Ship change-stream broadcaster behind feature flag, no clients connect
Step 3: Add 3D view as a separate route /fleet3d - both views coexist
Step 4: Soft-launch to ops team, gather feedback
Step 5: Make 3D the default; keep table view available via toggle
Step 6: After 3 months, remove the 30s-polling endpoint if usage is < 5%
testing_strategy: Performance test: render 5k instances on a CPU-throttled VM, assert avg frame time < 16ms using stats.js. Memory test: open and close FleetView3D 50 times, snapshot heap, assert no growth > 5MB. Socket batching test: simulate 100 sensor updates/sec, assert client receives at most 1 batch/sec. Disposal test: navigate away, assert THREE.WebGLRenderer.info.memory.geometries returns to baseline.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would render one <mesh> per sensor in r3f - 5k draw calls drops to 5fps. They'd update via React state, causing reconciliation of the entire tree on every update. They'd subscribe to raw change-stream events without coalescing, frying the network. They'd forget to dispose materials and geometries on unmount, leaking GPU memory across navigation.

title: Introduce organization-based multi-tenancy with tenant context middleware, DAL enforcement, and frontend scoping
business_context: Single-tenant SaaS has won three enterprise customers who insist on data isolation guarantees. Instead of running separate instances per customer, we need row-level multi-tenancy in shared DB. Existing 200 customers should be migrated transparently into a 'default' org. Future leakage bugs across tenants are unacceptable.
constraints: Cannot perform downtime migration - existing customers stay live
Frontend bundle size growth < 30KB
All existing API endpoints must continue working
Tenant isolation must be enforced even if a developer forgets to add the filter
Must support a single user belonging to multiple orgs

before_state.description: All collections have userId only. Queries filter by userId. No org concept.

issues: findById has no scoping - any caller knowing an id can read
No tenant boundary - if multi-org introduced, leakage is one missing where-clause away
Manual ownership check duplicated in every controller - easy to forget
Once orgs exist, single-userId ownership model breaks for shared resources
No org context in API calls - assumes single org per user
No way to switch active org

reasoning: Three layers of defense for tenant isolation: middleware injects tenant context from JWT, DAL refuses queries without tenant context via mongoose middleware, and a lint rule banning direct ProjectModel imports outside dal/. Resolving the active org: JWT contains user, but active org comes from X-Org-Id header validated against user's memberships. Middleware looks up membership and rejects if user not in that org. AsyncLocalStorage propagates the org through async layers - service and DAL don't need to thread it through every function. Mongoose pre-hook on find/findOne automatically adds {orgId} to filter, using context from AsyncLocalStorage. This is the safety net: even if a developer writes Model.findById(id), they get tenant-scoped query implicitly. Migration: add orgId field to all collections, default value 'default-org', backfill all existing rows. Each existing user becomes a member of 'default-org'. After backfill, make orgId required and remove default. Frontend: an OrgContext stores active org id; API client sends X-Org-Id automatically. Org switcher in header. Tradeoff vs separate DB per tenant: stronger isolation but ops nightmare with 200 customers. Tradeoff vs row-level only without AsyncLocalStorage: depends on developer discipline - rejected because explicit safety net is required.

after_state.description: Tenant context middleware, AsyncLocalStorage propagation, Mongoose hook enforces orgId filter automatically. Frontend has org switcher and scoped API client.

files.purpose: Resolves user's active org, validates membership, propagates context via AsyncLocalStorage
Mongoose model auto-applies orgId filter; throws if context missing - eliminates leakage risk
Repository methods don't take orgId - context provides it implicitly via Mongoose hook
Tracks active org, lists memberships, switches via full reload to clear cached data
API client transparently attaches X-Org-Id from localStorage on every request

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add Org + Membership models; create one 'default' org; create membership for every existing user
Step 2: Add orgId field to all tenant-scoped collections with default 'default-org'; backfill in batches
Step 3: Deploy tenant-context middleware and Mongoose hooks; existing users hit default org automatically
Step 4: Make orgId required and remove defaults; remove backwards-compat code
Step 5: Add org-switcher UI; onboard first enterprise customer into their own org
Step 6: Add lint rule: no direct ProjectModel/etc imports outside dal/ folder
Step 7: Add CI check that runs unscoped query test - asserts every model throws if no context
testing_strategy: Isolation test: create two orgs, two users in different orgs each with one project; assert user A querying any endpoint never sees user B's project (404, never 200). Static analysis: grep for Model.find calls in service/controller layers - none should exist. Load test with mixed-org traffic to verify no perf regression from extra index lookup. Migration dry-run on staging snapshot.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would add a where({orgId}) call in each repository function manually. This is the exact failure mode that caused the rewrite: one missed call leaks data. They'd also store activeOrgId in React state only, so it's lost on reload, and users see another org's data on cold load. They'd forget that JWTs already issued don't have org info, so existing sessions need a migration path.

title: Replace email/password auth with SSO-capable flow keeping local accounts for legacy users
business_context: Enterprise customers require SAML/OIDC SSO. Current auth is email/password only. 80% of new signups will be SSO, but 12k existing users have password accounts that must keep working. Need a unified user model and a pluggable identity provider system.
constraints: Existing users must not need to reset passwords
Migration must roll back cleanly if SSO provider integration fails
JWT and refresh-token contracts cannot change
Frontend login screen must adapt per domain (SSO discovery)

before_state.description: User has email + bcrypt passwordHash. Single login route. JWT issuance directly from controller.

issues: Auth strategy hardcoded - no abstraction for SSO
User schema assumes password - can't model SSO-only users
No notion of identity providers
No SSO discovery flow
Forces password input even for SSO-only users

reasoning: Introduce an Identity concept distinct from User. One User can have multiple Identity records: one of type 'password' (existing), one or more of type 'oidc'/'saml' (new). User profile (displayName, role, orgs) stays on User. Auth flow becomes: 1) frontend asks /api/auth/discover with email - server replies with provider type (password, oidc with redirect URL, etc.); 2) client initiates the correct flow; 3) callback or password POST creates a session and issues JWT. Token contract unchanged so downstream consumers don't care. Strategies pattern in service layer: PasswordStrategy, OidcStrategy, SamlStrategy, each implementing authenticate(input) -> User. Migration: existing users get a password identity created from passwordHash. Existing login endpoint becomes a thin shim that calls PasswordStrategy. New endpoints /api/auth/oidc/start and /api/auth/oidc/callback handle OIDC. Rollback: if SSO breaks, feature flag forces all logins to password path. Tradeoff vs library like Passport.js: pulls in 30+ deps, opinionated session handling that conflicts with our JWT approach. Building a small abstraction matches our needs.

after_state.description: Auth split into discovery, strategies, and token issuance. Identity model decoupled from User. Frontend adapts via discovery.

files.purpose: Identity decoupled from User - multiple per user allowed
Strategy pattern - each provider implements authenticate(input)->AuthResult; new providers slot in without touching controllers
Controllers stay thin; strategies handle method-specific logic; token issuance shared
Two-stage form - discover provider first, then password OR redirect to SSO

migration_strategy: Step 1: Create Identity collection; backfill one password-type identity per existing user from User.passwordHash
Step 2: Deploy new code with PasswordStrategy reading from Identity; existing login route still works
Step 3: Remove passwordHash from User; queries now use Identity
Step 4: Add OIDC provider config per email domain (acme.com -> Okta tenant); enable for first enterprise customer
Step 5: Add SAML strategy for second enterprise customer
Step 6: Monitor for 30 days; rollback path is feature flag DISABLE_SSO that forces all logins to password discovery
testing_strategy: Migration test: snapshot user table, run backfill, assert every user has exactly one password identity with matching hash. Strategy unit tests with mocked providers. End-to-end Playwright test: SSO flow with OIDC mock IdP. Backwards compat test: hit /api/auth/login with email+password (old route) - still returns valid JWT.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would add ssoToken field to User and a separate /api/auth/sso endpoint, leaving password code untouched. Result: two parallel auth code paths drift, account linking is impossible (two users with same email - one SSO, one password), and SAML adds a third path with the same issues. Identity model normalizes this.

title: Eliminate N+1 explosion in feed endpoint by adding aggregation pipeline and frontend virtualization
business_context: Home feed endpoint runs 1+N queries: list 50 posts, then loop fetching author + comment count + like count for each. p99 is 2.8s. Mobile users abandon the app waiting. Need single-roundtrip data and a frontend that can handle 500-item infinite scroll without freezing.
constraints: Cannot change response shape (mobile apps depend on it)
Must support cursor-based pagination
Memory of mobile clients limited - no rendering 500 DOM nodes at once
Order must remain createdAt DESC

before_state.description: Service makes per-post lookups in a loop. Frontend renders all loaded posts.

issues: 1 + 50*3 = 151 DB roundtrips per feed call
Each Promise.all map runs in parallel but still queries one record at a time
All loaded posts in DOM - 500 cards causes scroll lag
No virtualization - mobile freezes

reasoning: Backend: single aggregation pipeline with $lookup and $count subpipelines fetches everything in one roundtrip. The $lookup on counts uses pipeline with $count to avoid loading full arrays. Frontend: react-window or @tanstack/react-virtual for windowed rendering - only ~15 visible items live in DOM. IntersectionObserver triggers next page fetch. Tradeoff vs caching: a Redis cache hides the problem but breaks freshness; feeds need recency. Tradeoff vs denormalizing counters into Post document: requires consistency work and double-writes; for now keep counts queried but in one aggregation. Tradeoff vs GraphQL DataLoader: would solve N+1 but needs GraphQL layer - too much surface change for this fix.

after_state.description: Single aggregation pipeline materializes feed. Frontend uses virtualized list with intersection-based pagination.

files.purpose: One aggregation pipeline performs all joins and count subqueries - single DB roundtrip
Virtualized infinite scroll - only ~15 items in DOM regardless of total loaded count

migration_strategy: Step 1: Ship new aggregation behind feature flag FEED_AGGREGATION=false; old code unchanged
Step 2: Enable for 10% of users, monitor p99 and result correctness via shadow comparison
Step 3: Ramp to 100% and delete old code path
Step 4: Add virtualization on frontend behind a separate flag; ship to web first, then to embedded mobile webview
Step 5: After 30 days remove flags
testing_strategy: Snapshot test: same cursor returns identical posts in same order between old and new implementations. Load test: 100 RPS to feed endpoint, assert p99 < 500ms. Frontend perf: lighthouse score on feed page with 500 loaded posts asserts main thread blocked < 50ms. Virtualization regression test: scroll to bottom, assert all posts in window are visible and that DOM contains <30 cards.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would replace per-item queries with Promise.all of $in queries by author id - better but still 3 separate queries. They'd also forget to virtualize the frontend; the backend gets fast but rendering 500 cards still freezes mobile. Or they'd virtualize but with fixed item height, breaking when posts have varying content length.

title: Replace ad-hoc CORS and missing CSRF defenses with coherent same-site + token-based protection
business_context: A bug bounty report demonstrated CSRF on POST /api/account/email by tricking a logged-in user into visiting attacker.com which submits a form to our API. Current defenses are wildcard CORS in dev that leaked to prod via env mistake. Need same-site cookie strategy, double-submit CSRF token, and proper CORS without breaking the SPA or partner integrations.
constraints: Cannot break partner integrations using long-lived API keys
SPA on same eTLD+1 must work
Mobile app talking to API must work
No false positives on legitimate cross-origin reads of public endpoints

before_state.description: CORS allowed all origins. No CSRF token. Cookies are HttpOnly but SameSite default.

issues: origin: true reflects any origin - effectively wildcard with credentials
No CSRF protection at all
cookieParser without secret means signed cookies unavailable
State-changing endpoint with no CSRF protection - browser will send session cookie automatically from any origin
No CSRF token attached - needs to read it from cookie and send as header

reasoning: Layered defense: 1) Strict CORS allowlist from env, no reflection. Public read endpoints exposed via a separate router with a public-CORS middleware. 2) Same-site cookies: SameSite=Lax for auth cookie - browser won't send on top-level cross-site POSTs. 3) Double-submit CSRF token for state-changing endpoints when authenticated via cookies. Token is a random value set in a non-HttpOnly cookie + must be echoed in X-CSRF-Token header. Attacker cannot read the cookie (SOP) so cannot set the header. 4) Bearer-token clients (mobile, partners) bypass CSRF because they don't rely on cookies. Tradeoff vs SameSite=Strict: breaks all cross-tab navigation auth - rejected. Tradeoff vs synchronizer token pattern (per-session token stored server-side): more secure but requires server state and revocation logic; double-submit is sufficient with HTTPS-only.

after_state.description: Strict CORS allowlist, SameSite=Lax cookies, double-submit CSRF middleware skipped for bearer-token auth, frontend reads CSRF cookie and attaches header.

files.purpose: Two CORS strategies - strict for credentialed endpoints, permissive read-only for public ones
Double-submit CSRF - cookie + header must match. Bearer auth bypasses. Safe methods bypass.
App composition with route-group-specific middleware; CSRF only applied to authenticated/state-changing namespace
Client lazy-initializes CSRF cookie via GET endpoint and attaches X-CSRF-Token on every mutation

migration_strategy: Step 1: Tighten CORS to env-driven allowlist; deploy with allowlist matching all current origins (no behavior change yet)
Step 2: Deploy CSRF issuance only - cookie is set but verification is logging-only
Step 3: Audit logs to identify any legitimate cross-origin POSTers missing the header; reach out to fix
Step 4: Flip CSRF verification from log-only to enforce
Step 5: Set SameSite=Lax on auth cookie
Step 6: Roll out frontend client.ts changes; old POSTs without header now fail - users without the new bundle get logged out on next mutation
testing_strategy: CSRF regression test: POST without X-CSRF-Token returns 403, with matching token returns 200, with mismatched returns 403. Bearer test: POST with valid Bearer and no CSRF returns 200. CORS test: OPTIONS with unknown Origin gets no Access-Control-Allow-Origin. End-to-end Playwright test: simulate the original bug bounty exploit, verify it's blocked. Browser test on iframe-embedded scenarios used by the embed widget.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would add csurf middleware globally with no opt-out, breaking partner integrations using API keys. They might use SameSite=Strict, breaking the SSO redirect flow which is cross-site by design. Or they'd verify CSRF by checking the Origin header alone - which can be spoofed in some non-browser contexts and is missing in same-origin requests.

title: Refactor form state from individual useState fields to a controlled Zod-validated reducer with optimistic UI
business_context: User-settings page has 23 fields managed by 23 useState hooks. Validation logic is duplicated between client and server. Submitting fires 5 separate API calls in sequence; if one fails, partial save leaves UI inconsistent. Want a coherent form story usable across 8 settings pages.
constraints: Submission must be atomic - all-or-nothing
Validation errors must surface inline at the field
Saved state must persist until user navigates away even after a save
Cannot adopt a new form library if it adds > 25KB gzipped

before_state.description: Each field is its own useState. Submit runs sequential fetches. No shared form reducer.

issues: Five sequential network calls - any failure leaves state half-saved
No client validation
No dirty tracking - cannot warn on unsaved changes
23 individual useState calls cause re-render storms
Five endpoints encourage non-atomic frontend behavior
No shared validation - each endpoint does its own thing

reasoning: Frontend: introduce a useForm hook backed by useReducer. State shape mirrors a single Zod schema. The reducer handles SET_FIELD, SET_TOUCHED, START_SUBMIT, SUBMIT_SUCCESS, SUBMIT_ERROR. Validation runs on submit and on blur per field. Dirty tracking by comparing current state to initial snapshot. Backend: collapse five endpoints into one PATCH /api/settings that accepts a partial Settings DTO and performs all updates in a single Mongo transaction. Shared schema in /shared via Zod. Optimistic UI: on submit click, apply local state immediately, send to server, rollback to last-saved on error. Tradeoff vs react-hook-form: powerful but with adapters and a learning curve - the team already uses Zod and the form is simple field-name => value; a 60-line hook is cheaper. Tradeoff vs Formik: heavier and de-emphasized in 2025.

after_state.description: Single atomic endpoint with transactional update. Frontend useForm hook with Zod validation, dirty tracking, and optimistic UI.

files.purpose: Single source of truth for settings shape shared by client and server
Single endpoint applies all settings in one transaction - atomic save
Generic reducer-backed form hook - validation, dirty tracking, atomic submit, no external dependency
Settings page uses useForm - validation, dirty tracking, atomic submit, beforeunload warning

migration_strategy: Step 1: Ship shared schema and new /api/settings endpoint alongside existing 5 endpoints
Step 2: Migrate SettingsPage to useForm + new endpoint - old endpoints kept for other consumers
Step 3: Identify other consumers via API access logs; migrate each to new endpoint
Step 4: After 30 days of zero traffic to old endpoints, delete them
Step 5: Roll out useForm to 7 other settings pages one by one
testing_strategy: Atomicity test: simulate DB failure mid-transaction, assert no fields saved. Validation test: submit invalid values, assert field-level errors appear and no network call fires. Dirty tracking test: change field, navigate away, assert beforeunload warning. Optimistic UI test: simulate slow network, assert UI reflects new value immediately and rolls back on 500.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would add react-hook-form (acceptable) but skip the backend collapse, leaving the 5-endpoint problem unsolved - they'd still see partial-save bugs. Or they'd consolidate the backend but skip the transaction, so a Mongo failure on the third updateOne leaves first two saved.

title: Decompose monolithic Express controllers into Router→Controller→Service→DAL layers without breaking routes
business_context: A 1,200-line auth.controller.ts file holds routing, validation, business logic, and DB queries. Onboarding new engineers requires reading the whole file. Testing requires booting Mongo. Need to split into clean layers without big-bang rewrite.
constraints: URL paths and response shapes must not change
Tests must keep passing on each step
Splits must be reviewable in chunks < 500 LOC
Existing CI integration tests must remain green throughout

before_state.description: Single file mixes routing, validation, business logic, and DB access.

issues: Validation, business logic, persistence, and crypto all mixed
Cannot unit test without Mongo
Hard to add cross-cutting concerns like audit logging without touching every route

reasoning: Standard layering: Router declares paths and middleware. Controller adapts HTTP -> service call -> HTTP response. Service holds business logic and orchestrates DAL. DAL wraps Mongoose. Migration: extract one route at a time. Keep old src/auth.ts file in place until all routes extracted, then delete. Each route extraction is a single PR. Each layer has its own test seam: services tested with in-memory DAL fakes; controllers tested by injecting mock services. Tradeoff vs hexagonal/clean architecture: too many layers for a CRUD service; 4 layers is enough. Tradeoff vs collocating by feature (all auth files in src/features/auth/): equally valid; chosen for parallelism with existing folder structure.

after_state.description: Four-layer split: router, controller, service, DAL. Each layer testable in isolation.

files.purpose: Router declares paths and middleware; no business logic
Controllers adapt HTTP <-> service; HTTP status mapping lives here
Service holds business rules and orchestrates DAL + crypto; throws domain error names, not HTTP statuses
DAL wraps Mongoose with a stable API - tests can mock this module to avoid DB

migration_strategy: Step 1: Create empty new files (router, controller, service, DAL) for one route - register
Step 2: Move register logic into new files; old file still mounts old route - new files mounted in parallel under prefix /_new
Step 3: Run integration tests against both old and new paths to verify parity
Step 4: Swap router to mount the new path under /api/auth, remove old route from old file
Step 5: Repeat for each remaining route in old file
Step 6: Delete old src/auth.ts when empty
Step 7: Add ESLint rule preventing import of Mongoose models from outside dal/ folder
testing_strategy: Snapshot tests on response shape for each endpoint - run before and after every step. Unit tests added per layer: service tests mock userRepo, controller tests mock authService. Integration tests on the router still run against real Mongo. Each PR runs full test suite plus a grep check that no business logic remains in the old file.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would do the rewrite as one giant PR, making review impossible and tests untrustworthy. Or they'd leave the service layer thin and put business logic in controllers, defeating the purpose. Or they'd not introduce a DAL layer, leaving controllers/services with direct Mongoose calls and no test seam.

title: Add typing indicators with hierarchical rate limiting and back-pressure handling
business_context: Users requested typing indicators in chat. Quick prototype emitted 60 events/sec while typing, melting the server at 1k concurrent rooms. Need a production-grade implementation that's responsive but doesn't generate unbounded events.
constraints: Indicator must appear within 300ms of someone starting to type
Indicator must disappear within 3s of stopping
Server must handle 10k concurrent rooms with 5 typers each
Rate limit must be coordinated across multiple instances
Stale indicators after disconnect must clear automatically

before_state.description: Chat namespace exists with messages. No typing concept.

issues: No typing event handling
No rate limiting on any event
No typing notification at all

reasoning: Client side: emit typing:start once when user types in an idle state, then a typing:stop after 3s of inactivity OR on message send. Use a debounced/throttled approach - don't emit on every keystroke. Server side: token-bucket rate limit per (socket, room) - max 2 typing:start per 5s. Server also broadcasts typing:stop based on a per-user TTL stored in Redis - if no refresh in 4s, server auto-emits stop. This handles disconnects and dropped stop events. Coordination across instances: Redis adapter already exists; typing state is in Redis with TTL, and a single instance per room runs a periodic sweep to emit expirations. Tradeoff vs running sweep in every instance: causes duplicate emits. Solution: deterministic ownership - hash room id to one instance via consistent hashing on a known instance list. Too complex for v1 - simpler: every instance sweeps, broadcasts include a debounce key, client de-dupes. Frontend: Set of typing user ids per room, debounced render.

after_state.description: Throttled client emits, server token bucket, Redis TTL with auto-stop, frontend debounced indicator UI.

files.purpose: Token-bucket rate limit per socket; Redis stores typing state with TTL; disconnect clears all rooms
Client-side throttle: emit start once on first activity, stop after 3s of idle, force stop on unmount
Input wires activity to throttled emitter and renders indicator from socket-derived state

migration_strategy: Step 1: Deploy server typing handler behind socket-level feature flag; no clients use it
Step 2: Roll out frontend useTypingIndicator hook to one chat surface
Step 3: Load test with simulated 10k rooms; monitor Redis ops/sec
Step 4: Enable for all users
Step 5: Set up alert on typing event rate exceeding 1k/sec/instance
testing_strategy: Rate limit test: rapidly type to emit 20 typing:start in 5s, assert at most 4 reach the server. Disconnect test: connect, emit typing:start, kill socket, assert peers receive typing:stop within 1s. Multi-instance test: emit from instance A, verify peer on instance B sees indicator via Redis adapter. Auto-cleanup test: emit typing:start with no follow-up, assert peer's indicator clears within 4.5s.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would emit typing:start on every onChange. With 50 users in a room each typing 5 chars/sec that's 250 events/sec/room. Multiplied across rooms it saturates the server. They'd also forget the TTL auto-clean, leading to stuck indicators when network drops between start and stop.

title: Build interactive 3D floor plan with real-time desk booking via WebSockets
business_context: Hybrid office wants employees to book desks. Current system is a 2D HTML grid that fails to convey which area of the floor the desk is in. New view: top-down 3D floor plan, click a desk to book, see live availability without refresh. 800 desks per floor across 4 floors.
constraints: Must run on Chromebooks used by office admins
Booking conflicts (two users clicking same desk) must be resolved server-side
Camera must remain locked to top-down with limited zoom/pan
Visual feedback for own booking vs others' bookings
Three-second p99 to reflect a peer's booking

before_state.description: 2D HTML grid via CSS; bookings via REST polling every 30s.

issues: Polling - 30s lag
No spatial layout
Race: two users click same desk and both see success before refresh
Read-then-write race - two concurrent requests both see bookedBy null and both save
No realtime broadcast

reasoning: Server: replace read-then-write with conditional updateOne that only sets bookedBy if it's null - this gives atomic claim. If 0 docs matched, someone else got it. On success, broadcast desk:booked to floor room. Frontend 3D: r3f canvas with OrbitControls limited to pan-only (lock polar angle and zoom min/max). Each desk is an instanced mesh - one geometry, position per desk, color per state. Click via raycaster on individual mesh group. Subscribe to floor room on mount; update instance color on incoming events. Visual feedback: own bookings get a different color and a small icon above. Tradeoff vs sprites for desks: instancing is needed because 800 desks per floor. Tradeoff vs WebGPU: not supported on Chromebooks. Stay on WebGL2.

after_state.description: Server atomic-claim. Socket broadcast on book/unbook. 3D floor plan with locked top-down camera and instanced desks responding to live updates.

files.purpose: Atomic conditional updates eliminate race; socket broadcast notifies floor occupants
Locked top-down camera; pan and zoom allowed but no rotation
Instanced desks colored by state, raycaster delivers instanceId for click handling
Initial fetch + socket subscription + optimistic UI with rollback on server reject

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add x/z coords to Desk schema; backfill from existing grid coordinates
Step 2: Ship atomic-claim and socket broadcast - old 2D grid continues working with REST + polling
Step 3: Add /floor3d route alongside old grid - both live
Step 4: A/B test for two weeks
Step 5: Make 3D the default; keep grid for accessibility users
Step 6: Add ARIA fallback list of desks for screen readers
testing_strategy: Race test: two parallel requests to book same desk - assert exactly one succeeds with 200, other gets 409. Realtime test: book in browser A, assert browser B sees desk turn red within 3s. Disposal test: navigate away and back, assert no leaked WebGL contexts via renderer.info. Click test: click free desk, assert it turns blue (own booking) and POST fires.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would still do read-then-write in the controller, creating a race where both users get success and the last write wins. They'd render 800 separate <mesh> nodes in r3f, costing 800 draw calls. They'd forget to limit OrbitControls polar angle, letting users tip the camera and lose orientation.

title: Add per-tenant theming via design tokens with server-controlled palette and client-side runtime
business_context: Enterprise SSO customers want their branding on the app - logo, primary color, accent. Currently styles are hardcoded in Tailwind. Need server-controlled tokens applied at runtime, default fallbacks, and ability to preview before publishing.
constraints: No Tailwind rebuild per tenant - tokens must be runtime CSS variables
Must support light/dark mode independently per tenant
Preview must not affect other users in the same org
Bundle size growth < 5KB

before_state.description: Tailwind config has fixed primary color. No tenant theming. Components use bg-blue-600 etc.

issues: Single global palette
No way to change colors per tenant
Hardcoded brand color - cannot change per org
No theme fields on Org

reasoning: Use CSS custom properties driven by server-provided tokens. Tailwind config maps semantic tokens (primary, surface, etc.) to var(--color-primary). Component classes reference these semantic tokens, not blue-600. On login or org switch, frontend fetches /api/org/:id/theme and applies CSS vars to :root via a style tag. Dark mode handled by separate token sets (theme.light, theme.dark) - swap based on user preference. Preview: editor sets vars only on a #preview-root element, not :root, so other tabs unaffected. Server: Org doc has theme JSON. Validation enforces hex colors and luminance contrast. Tradeoff vs CSS-in-JS: runtime cost, harder to debug. Tradeoff vs separate Tailwind builds per tenant: scales poorly. Tradeoff vs server-rendered SSR styles: this is a client SPA; vars work fine.

after_state.description: Tokens defined in Tailwind via CSS vars. Theme service applies vars on org load. Preview scoped to subtree.

files.purpose: Semantic color tokens point to CSS vars - swappable at runtime
Default token values; dark class scopes overrides
Provider applies tenant theme on org change; ThemePreview scopes vars to subtree for editor preview
Components reference semantic tokens, not literal colors
Theme endpoints with Zod-validated hex colors; default returned if none set

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add CSS vars and update Tailwind config; existing classes (bg-blue-600 etc.) still work because vars only define new tokens
Step 2: Migrate components leaf-up: Button, Card, Nav swap to semantic tokens
Step 3: Add ThemeProvider to app root - applies default theme = current colors
Step 4: Ship theme editor for org admins; first enterprise customer customizes
Step 5: After all components migrated, ban literal Tailwind color utility classes via lint rule
Step 6: Drop legacy Tailwind blue palette utilities if needed
testing_strategy: Visual regression: snapshot Button before and after migration with default theme - must be pixel-identical. Theming test: apply custom theme, assert computed background-color of button matches. Preview isolation test: render ThemePreview with green theme alongside default-themed sibling, assert sibling colors unchanged. Contrast test: theme schema rejects (foreground, background) pair with WCAG AA contrast < 4.5.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would maintain separate Tailwind builds per tenant - infeasible to ship and bloats CDN. Or they'd use inline style props on every component - styled-components-style mess that breaks Tailwind's hover/focus modifiers. Or they'd set CSS vars without semantic abstraction, requiring every component to reference --color-primary directly with no fallback chain.

title: Add idempotent webhook receiver with replay protection and migrate existing inline payment handler
business_context: Stripe sends webhook events at-least-once; the current handler updates orders directly without dedup, occasionally double-charging credits. Need idempotency, signature verification, and durable retry semantics. Existing inline handler is in the payment.controller, mixed with business logic.
constraints: Cannot lose events during deploy
Stripe expects 2xx within 5 seconds or retries
Must handle replays from manual operator triggers
Database is Mongo with multi-doc transactions available

before_state.description: Webhook handler validates signature inline, updates Order in same request. No event dedup, no separation of acceptance from processing.

issues: No idempotency - replay credits user twice
Heavy work in webhook handler risks 5s timeout
Mixes acceptance and processing

reasoning: Two-step pattern: 1) Receiver verifies signature, persists raw event to InboundEvent collection with stripe event id as _id (natural dedup), returns 2xx immediately. 2) Worker scans InboundEvent.status='pending', processes each via type handler in a Mongo transaction, marks 'processed' or 'failed'. Idempotency: handler also writes to a IdempotencyKey collection per (eventId, sideEffect) to make even worker retries safe. If worker crashes mid-transaction, the doc remains pending and is retried. Tradeoff vs Kafka/SQS: introduces infra; Mongo collection suffices for our volume (< 1k events/day). Tradeoff vs synchronous processing with retries inside the request: keeps the bug-prone coupling.

after_state.description: Webhook receiver only verifies and persists. Worker processes inbox in transactions with idempotency keys.

files.purpose: Inbox table; natural dedup via source-provided id as _id
Receiver verifies signature and writes inbox row keyed by event id; duplicate inserts swallowed; ack immediately
Worker polls inbox, processes each event in a transaction with idempotency keys, marks done or retries up to 10 times

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add inbox model and worker behind feature flag NEW_WEBHOOK_PIPELINE=false; old inline handler runs
Step 2: Flip new pipeline on; webhook handler becomes receiver; worker reads inbox
Step 3: Backfill: replay last 7 days of stripe events into inbox via Stripe API, mark as processed (since old code already handled them)
Step 4: Monitor inbox depth - alert if > 100 pending
Step 5: Remove old inline handler after 30 days of stability
testing_strategy: Idempotency test: process the same event twice, assert order paid exactly once, credit created exactly once. Replay test: trigger Stripe replay (or simulate by reposting same event id), assert ack 200 and no double processing. Crash test: kill worker mid-transaction, restart, assert event eventually processed exactly once. Signature test: send event with bad signature, assert 400 and no inbox row.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would add an if-already-paid check in the controller. Doesn't help if credit creation succeeded but order update failed - retry double-credits. They'd skip transactions, leaving partial-state windows. They'd skip the inbox, doing all work in the request - 5s timeout causes Stripe to retry, multiplying side effects.

title: Fix Socket.IO memory leak from forgotten listeners; introduce per-socket cleanup discipline
business_context: Server RSS grows from 200MB to 4GB over 24h then OOMs. Heap snapshot reveals millions of EventEmitter listener entries tied to Socket.IO. Root cause: services subscribe to messageBus inside socket connect handlers but never unsubscribe. Need a systemic fix.
constraints: Cannot redeploy multiple times during business hours
Must keep all existing socket features working
Cannot use Node restart as a band-aid

before_state.description: Each socket connection registers a listener on the global event bus and never removes it.

issues: Listener never removed on disconnect - leaks per connection
Filter on userId means each event runs N listeners - O(N) for N connected users
Listener registered on every order:watch call - multiple per socket
No removal on disconnect
Inner closure captures orderId so it can't even be removed by identity

reasoning: Two complementary fixes. First, the leak: every socket-level listener must be cleaned up. Wrap socket lifecycle in a helper that tracks listeners and removes them on disconnect. Second, the structural problem: filtering inside a global listener per socket is O(N) at emit time. Replace with room-based dispatch: socket joins room `user:${userId}` on connect; bus subscribers emit to that room only. One bus listener per process, fan-out by room - O(1) at emit time. Tradeoff vs leaving filtering in each socket but cleaning up: still O(N). Room dispatch eliminates the per-socket bus listener entirely. Tradeoff vs RxJS observables: powerful but a new dep for a fix; native Map of cleanup fns is enough.

after_state.description: Single bus listener per namespace; sockets get updates via room membership. Per-socket cleanup utility for any remaining direct subscriptions.

files.purpose: Helper that auto-removes listeners and side effects on socket disconnect
Single bus listener per process; per-user delivery via room - constant work at emit time
Sockets join order rooms on watch; one bus listener fans out via rooms; cleanup tracked by lifecycle helper

migration_strategy: Step 1: Hotfix: deploy room-based dispatch immediately during business hours - safe because behavior is identical, just routed differently
Step 2: After memory stabilizes, audit other namespaces for the same pattern; apply withCleanup
Step 3: Add ESLint rule: forbid messageBus.on() inside socket connect handlers - require withCleanup or namespace-level subscription
Step 4: Add memory regression test in CI: open 1000 sockets, disconnect all, assert listener count returns to baseline
Step 5: Document the pattern in CONTRIBUTING.md
testing_strategy: Listener leak test: open and close 1000 sockets, assert messageBus.listenerCount('user.notification') stays at 1. Load test for 1h with simulated churn, monitor RSS - assert no upward trend. Behavioral test: emit user.notification, assert the targeted user's socket receives it and others do not.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would add socket.on('disconnect') to remove the listener - works if the listener reference is captured. But if the listener is an inline arrow function, .off won't match and removal fails silently. Or they'd switch to once() - works for single-event listeners but not for the stream-like 'notification' case. Room-based dispatch sidesteps the problem entirely.

title: Rate limit auth endpoints by IP+account+device with sliding window backed by Redis
business_context: Brute force attempts increased 40x last week. Current rate limit is express-rate-limit per IP with 100 req/15min - too coarse and bypassed by IP rotation. Need multi-dimension rate limits: per IP, per account, per device fingerprint, with sliding window precision.
constraints: Must work across multiple Node instances
Failed logins must lock the account temporarily after 5 failures in 5min
Must not lock out a legitimate user from a single device after a bad guess
Must integrate with existing auth flow without API change
Audit log must capture all lockout events

before_state.description: Single express-rate-limit per IP. Lockouts not enforced. In-memory state lost on restart.

issues: IP-only granularity
Fixed window allows bursts at window edges
In-memory store doesn't share across instances
No tracking of failure count per account
No exponential backoff
Successful login doesn't reset failure counter

reasoning: Sliding window rate limit using Redis sorted sets: each request adds a member with score = timestamp; count members with score > now - windowMs. Atomic via Lua script to avoid race. Multi-dimension: separate keys for ip:, acct:, dev: prefixes, each with own limit. Request denied if any dimension exceeds. Lockout: account-level counter tracks consecutive failures; after 5 in 5min, return 423 until 15min passes. Device fingerprint: hash of UA+screen+lang sent by client; weaker but adds another dimension that's costly for attackers to rotate. Tradeoff vs token bucket: sliding window precise for security/audit; token bucket allows bursts which can be exploited. Tradeoff vs leaky bucket: more complex math; sorted set is simple and correct.

after_state.description: Sliding-window Lua-scripted Redis limiter with three dimensions. Lockout state in Redis with TTL. Audit on every block.

files.purpose: Atomic sliding-window via Lua; one key per dimension; expiration tied to window
Lockout state in Redis; success resets counter; audit on lockout
Three independent sliding-window limiters + account lockout chained before controller
Controller wires success/failure into the lockout counter
Lightweight client fingerprint sent as X-Device-Fp header to give server a third dimension

migration_strategy: Step 1: Deploy limiter middleware in log-only mode - records would-block events but does not deny
Step 2: Tune thresholds based on legitimate-traffic data observed
Step 3: Switch to enforce mode for IP dimension first
Step 4: Add account-lockout dimension; communicate to support team so they know how to unlock
Step 5: Add device fingerprint dimension; ship frontend update sending header
Step 6: Remove old express-rate-limit middleware
testing_strategy: Brute force test: simulate 50 login attempts from 5 IPs at the same email - assert lockout triggers at attempt 5. Distributed test: send requests via 50 different IPs against one account - assert account-dimension limit kicks in even though no single IP exceeds. Burst test at window edges: send 30 requests at t=0 then 30 at t=59s - assert sliding window blocks but old fixed-window would allow. Multi-instance test: verify limit shared across two Node instances via Redis.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would only add per-IP limits, allowing IP-rotating attackers through. Or they'd use the in-memory express-rate-limit MemoryStore with multiple instances, getting wildly inaccurate counts. Or they'd lock accounts permanently on failure, creating a denial-of-service vector where attackers lock victims out forever.

title: Replace prop-drilled auth context with split read/write contexts and lazy bootstrap
business_context: App passes user, login, logout, token through 9 levels of props in some routes. Every login or refresh causes deep re-renders even for components that only need user.id. Need split contexts and a bootstrap that avoids flash-of-unauthenticated-content on page load.
constraints: Cannot regress login UX
Bundle size budget: no new top-level deps
Must work with React 19 strict mode
Logout must immediately invalidate cached API responses

before_state.description: Single AuthContext with everything. Prop drilling for deep components. Login state lost on page reload until /me hits.

issues: Every consumer re-renders when token or loading changes, even if it only reads user.id
Flash of unauthenticated content before /me resolves
Logout doesn't clear cached responses elsewhere

reasoning: Split into AuthStateContext (user + loading) and AuthActionsContext (login + logout). Actions context value is stable (memoized) so action consumers never re-render. State context updates cause re-renders only for state consumers. Bootstrap: synchronously read user from sessionStorage if present, mark as 'optimistic'. Validate via /me in background; if it fails, downgrade to logged-out. This avoids flash for returning users. Logout: clear sessionStorage and emit a custom event that the API client listens to in order to abort in-flight requests and clear its cache. Tradeoff vs Zustand or Redux: existing app is on Context; introducing global store for one slice is overkill. Tradeoff vs subscribing to storage events for multi-tab logout: yes - add storage listener too. Tradeoff vs cookies for token: refresh token in HttpOnly cookie, access token in memory + sessionStorage for fast bootstrap. Cookies HttpOnly so JS can't read - bootstrap relies on /me call. Use sessionStorage for non-secret user shape to enable optimistic bootstrap.

after_state.description: Split contexts. Stable actions. Optimistic bootstrap from sessionStorage. Cross-tab logout via storage event.

files.purpose: Read-only state context - re-renders on auth state change
Stable actions - identity never changes so consumers never re-render from this
Provider with optimistic bootstrap, cross-tab logout, stable actions identity, and cache clear
Minimal cache cleared on logout to prevent stale user data leaking across sessions

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add new contexts side-by-side with old AuthContext; old code keeps working
Step 2: Migrate leaf components (Logout button, UserBadge) to new hooks
Step 3: Migrate middle layers; remove prop drilling
Step 4: Switch root from old AuthProvider to new
Step 5: Delete old AuthContext
testing_strategy: Re-render count test using @testing-library hooks - assert a component using useAuthActions does not re-render on user change. Bootstrap test: prefill sessionStorage, mount app, assert user visible before /me resolves. Cross-tab test: open two tabs, logout in one, assert second tab logs out within 100ms. Cache test: GET /me, then logout, then login as different user; assert /me returns new user (cache not stale).
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would just memoize the value of the single context with useMemo. Doesn't help - useMemo returns a new object whenever any dep changes, so all consumers still re-render on any auth field change. Splitting contexts is the structural fix. They'd also forget cross-tab and ship a confusing UX where one tab is logged out and another isn't.

title: Add live form co-editing with field-level locks, conflict-free updates, and visual peer indicators
business_context: Sales team co-edits opportunity forms - currently last-save-wins, causing data loss. Need field-level locks so two people can't edit the same field, real-time value updates so collaborators see typing, and clear ownership indicators.
constraints: Cannot block reading the form just because someone is editing
Locks must auto-release after 10s of inactivity
Network drops must not strand a lock indefinitely
Must survive page reload mid-edit
Form has 25 fields

before_state.description: Standard form with PATCH on save. No realtime, no locking.

issues: No awareness of other editors
Last-save-wins overwrites concurrent edits
Whole-document overwrite - field-level updates not respected

reasoning: Lock model: Redis SET with NX + EX per (oppId, fieldName) keyed `lock:opp:${id}:${field}` value = userId. Acquire on focus, release on blur or save. TTL ensures auto-release. Heartbeat extends TTL while editing. Server emits field:locked / field:unlocked. Field updates: client emits field:change with new value as user types (debounced 200ms). Server validates lock ownership before broadcasting. Conflict resolution: only lock owner can write a field; others see live value but cannot edit. Persistence: server writes to DB on debounced timer or on save. Reload: cleared local state but server returns current values + active locks on join. Tradeoff vs OT/CRDT (Yjs): heavyweight for a structured form. Field-level locks are sufficient because conflicts in opportunity records are typically across fields (sales rep edits notes, manager edits value).

after_state.description: Field-level locking via Redis, live value broadcast, lock-ownership-gated edits, auto-release on disconnect.

files.purpose: Lock acquire/release/heartbeat with auto-cleanup, lock-gated field updates broadcast to peers
Hook combines lock lifecycle, heartbeats, value broadcast, and computed canEdit per field
Field component shows peer editor name, disables when locked by someone else, acquires lock on focus

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add opportunity namespace, lock model in Redis; frontend hook behind feature flag
Step 2: Enable for 3-user pilot team; observe lock hand-off
Step 3: Roll out to all sales teams
Step 4: Add unlock-all admin command for stuck locks (e.g., browser crash)
Step 5: After 30 days, remove old whole-form save in favor of field-by-field persistence
testing_strategy: Lock contention test: two users focus same field simultaneously, assert exactly one succeeds. Disconnect test: acquire lock, kill browser, assert lock auto-releases within 12s. Heartbeat test: keep field focused for 60s, assert lock TTL never expires. Conflict-write test: bypass UI and emit field:change without lock, assert server ignores it.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would lock the whole form on edit. Painful UX - one user can't update notes while another updates value. They'd skip TTL/heartbeat, leaving stuck locks after browser crash. They'd trust client to release locks - attacker can prevent others from editing by holding a lock forever.

title: Migrate request-scoped DI from explicit threading to AsyncLocalStorage context with backward-compat shim
business_context: Currently services receive { db, logger, requestId, user } as explicit first arg. 200 service functions thread these through. Adding a new context field (orgId for multi-tenancy) requires touching every service signature. Need ambient context without losing type safety or testability.
constraints: Cannot break existing services in one PR
Type safety must remain - no any
Tests must continue working without setting up Express
No degradation in performance

before_state.description: Every service function takes ServiceContext as first arg. Callers thread it through.

issues: Threading context through every call is noisy
Every method takes ctx - adding a field requires updating 200 signatures
Forgetting to pass ctx is a silent bug

reasoning: AsyncLocalStorage stores context per async chain. Express middleware enters the store at request start. Services call getContext() inside their bodies. No more threading. Backward compat: keep accepting optional ctx arg; if provided use it, else fall back to ALS. Migrate services one at a time. Tests: an unsafe enterContext(ctx, fn) helper for unit tests. Tradeoff vs dependency injection container (tsyringe etc.): heavier, requires decorators, often awkward in TS strict. ALS is built-in and idiomatic. Performance: ALS has measured ~5% overhead per async hop vs explicit pass; acceptable. Tradeoff vs zone.js style: ALS is the official Node.js API; zone is dying.
