Type safety: getContext() returns ServiceContext or throws if missing - controllers/middleware guarantee it's set.

after_state.description: ALS-backed ambient context. Services call getContext(). Backward-compat shim accepts optional explicit ctx during migration.

files.purpose: ALS-backed ambient context with getContext (strict), tryGetContext (lenient), enterContext (for tests)
Middleware enters ALS scope per request; downstream handlers and services see the context
Methods read context via ALS by default; accept explicit ctx as escape hatch during migration
Test entry into ALS scope - no Express needed

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add context module, middleware, enter ALS on every request; existing services still receive ctx explicitly
Step 2: Migrate one service file at a time to call getContext(); leave optional ctx arg as escape hatch
Step 3: Update callers (controllers) to stop threading ctx where downstream services are migrated
Step 4: After all services migrated, remove the optional ctx arg from method signatures
Step 5: Add lint rule banning new ServiceContext arg additions
testing_strategy: Migration parity test: pick a service, run integration tests before and after migration - identical behavior. Concurrency test: 100 parallel requests with different orgIds, assert each sees its own context (no leak between requests). Test-without-Express harness uses enterContext to drive services directly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would use globals or a singleton context - races between concurrent requests, every async hop risks losing isolation. Or they'd use cls-hooked (a community shim) instead of native AsyncLocalStorage - extra dep, slower, deprecated. Or they'd skip the optional legacy arg and break 200 callers in one PR.

title: Visualize live order pipeline as 3D Sankey diagram with backend stage counts via change streams
business_context: Operations exec wants a 'pulse' view of order flow: how many orders are at each stage (pending → paid → packed → shipped → delivered → returned), with live counts and visual flow between stages. Currently this is a static table refreshed manually.
constraints: Must update within 2s of an order changing stage
60fps on a 4K display
Must not require running aggregations on every update
Stage transitions in the last 60s should animate

before_state.description: Static table view with a manual refresh button.

issues: Manual refresh - no live view
No flow visualization
Full-collection scan on every call
No transition tracking

reasoning: Backend: maintain a PipelineCounters singleton doc updated by change streams on Order. When an order is inserted, $inc counters.[status]. When updateDescription includes status, $inc the new bucket and $dec the old. Track recent transitions in a TTL collection with insertion time + from/to. Endpoint /api/ops/pipeline returns the counters doc; /api/ops/transitions returns last 60s transitions. Broadcast updates via Socket.IO at most 1Hz (debounced). Frontend: r3f with custom geometry per pipeline stage - boxes positioned along x axis sized by count. Flow lines between stages use TubeGeometry along curves; transitions in last 60s spawn particles moving along curves with GSAP. LOD: distant stages get fewer particles. Disposal handled with useEffect cleanup. Tradeoff vs D3 SVG: works but doesn't scale visually; 3D gives depth and impact for an exec dashboard. Tradeoff vs WebGL via Pixi.js: 2D engine - no perspective; r3f is the right tool here.

after_state.description: Change-stream-driven counter doc, transition history, Socket.IO push, 3D pipeline view with animated particles.

files.purpose: Change-stream maintains counts and recent transitions; debounced broadcast at 1Hz
Top-level canvas with stages, flows, controlled camera
Stage columns scaled by share of total - visually conveys distribution
Particles arc between stages over 1s real time; geometry and material disposed on completion and unmount
Hook subscribes to ops namespace, returns latest counts and recent transitions

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add PipelineCounter and Transition models; run change-stream projector to populate from existing data (seed from a one-time aggregation)
Step 2: Deploy backend changes and verify counters match aggregation snapshot
Step 3: Add new /ops/pipeline-3d route alongside table view
Step 4: Roll out to exec team
Step 5: Once stable, remove manual refresh table
testing_strategy: Projector correctness: simulate sequence of inserts and status updates, assert final counts match aggregation. Disposal test: open and close Pipeline3D 50 times, assert WebGL geometries return to baseline. Particle bounds test: simulate 1000 transitions/sec for 10s, assert particle count never exceeds reasonable threshold (e.g., 200 concurrent).
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would call the aggregation every 2s - works but loads primary as collection grows. They'd render flow particles via React state per particle - re-renders all of them every frame causing jank. They'd forget to dispose particle geometries, leaking GPU memory until tab crash.

title: Add API key authentication scoped to specific tenants alongside existing JWT user auth
business_context: Enterprise customers want server-to-server API access via API keys. Currently only user JWTs work. Need keys scoped to a single org with permission scopes (read/write per resource), key rotation, and revocation. JWT user auth continues unchanged.
constraints: Keys must hash at rest
Issuing a key returns it once; cannot recover it later
Permission scopes must be enforceable in middleware
Existing user JWT flow must remain untouched
Audit log of every key use

before_state.description: Only JWT auth in middleware. No API key model.

issues: Only JWTs accepted
No way to issue keys for server clients

reasoning: Hybrid auth middleware checks for both Bearer JWT and a prefix-based API key format (sk_live_xxxx). API keys stored as bcrypt hash + first-8 prefix for fast lookup. Each key has orgId and scopes array (e.g., ['orders:read','orders:write']). Route-level middleware requires specific scopes. Key issuance returns the full key once - subsequent retrievals only show metadata. Rotation: issue a new key, mark old one as deprecated, both work until grace period ends. Audit: every key use logged with key id (not the key itself). Tradeoff vs OAuth client credentials: more complex; opaque keys are sufficient for direct API access. Tradeoff vs HMAC-signed requests: more secure but harder for customers to implement; offer as advanced option later. Tradeoff vs lookup by full key hash: requires hashing every request - slower than prefix lookup + bcrypt compare on candidates.

after_state.description: Auth middleware accepts JWT or API key. Keys hashed at rest with prefix lookup. Route-level scope enforcement.

files.purpose: Key metadata with hashed secret; prefix enables fast candidate lookup
Issue returns plaintext once; verify uses prefix+bcrypt; lastUsedAt updated async
Auth middleware accepts JWT or API key; requireScope enforces per-route scope for API keys
Routes declare scope requirements - API key callers must have explicit scope; users go through role-based access
UI to issue keys (showing secret once), list metadata, and revoke

migration_strategy: Step 1: Ship ApiKey model and service alongside existing auth; no routes use scopes yet
Step 2: Add requireScope middleware to one read endpoint; verify with a test API key
Step 3: Add settings UI for issuing keys
Step 4: Roll out scope enforcement across all API endpoints
Step 5: Document API key usage in customer docs
testing_strategy: Issue+verify round-trip: create key, present it, assert verification succeeds and matches org. Wrong scope test: key with orders:read attempts POST /orders, assert 403. Revoked key test: revoke then present, assert 401. Concurrent verify test: 100 parallel verify calls with same key - should not corrupt lastUsedAt or fail.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would store API keys plaintext for ease of debugging - if DB leaks, all keys compromised. They'd hash the whole key with bcrypt and try to look up by hash - bcrypt is slow and salted differently each time so direct lookup is impossible. Prefix indexing is the key insight. They'd also forget the 'show once' UX, leading to keys stored in browser history.

title: Add file uploads with virus scanning, S3, and presigned URLs replacing existing in-memory upload
business_context: Current upload endpoint loads files into Node memory via multer, saves to local disk. Files lost on each deploy because containers ephemeral. No scanning. Need durable storage with virus scanning before user can access files, supporting up to 500MB videos.
constraints: Cannot serve infected files even briefly
Must work across pod restarts
No node memory pressure - cannot load 500MB into memory
Existing API contract: POST /api/upload returns {url} - must keep
Frontend progress UI must continue to work

before_state.description: Multer in-memory storage. Disk persistence. No scanning.

issues: 500MB file = 500MB Node memory
Local disk lost on container restart
No virus scan
Synchronous fs.writeFileSync blocks event loop
Sends whole file through Node API server
No status for virus scan

reasoning: Switch to presigned PUT URLs - frontend uploads directly to S3, bypassing Node. Backend issues short-lived presigned URL on request. On completion, frontend notifies backend which records the file metadata and triggers async scan job. Scanner (ClamAV) reads from S3, marks status 'clean' or 'infected'. Files marked 'pending' or 'infected' are not served. Reads via signed-URL endpoint that checks status. Progress: presigned PUTs support progress natively via XHR. Status: frontend polls or subscribes to socket for scan completion. API contract: original returns {url} immediately - now needs intermediate states. Solution: keep POST /api/upload as a wrapper that returns {fileId, uploadUrl, finalUrl}. Existing callers using {url} migrate to {finalUrl}. Tradeoff vs streaming through Node: works but uses Node bandwidth and memory. Tradeoff vs Lambda@Edge scanning: AWS-specific; ClamAV in a worker container is more portable.

after_state.description: Presigned S3 PUTs, async scanning, signed-URL access gated by scan status, socket notification on scan completion.

files.purpose: Three-step flow: init (presigned PUT), complete (queue scan), get (signed GET only if clean)
Worker fetches from S3, runs clamdscan, updates status, deletes infected files, notifies via socket
Three-stage UI: presigned upload to S3 with progress, scan wait via socket, final URL once clean

migration_strategy: Step 1: Provision S3 bucket and ClamAV worker container
Step 2: Ship new endpoints alongside old; old /api/upload endpoint still works
Step 3: Frontend uses new endpoints behind feature flag; legacy XHR-to-Node path used as fallback
Step 4: Migrate existing /var/uploads files to S3; rewrite stored URLs
Step 5: Remove old endpoint after 30 days of zero traffic
testing_strategy: Upload happy path: 500MB file uploads successfully without Node memory spike. Infected file test: upload EICAR test file, assert status becomes 'infected' and S3 object deleted. Resume test: kill scan worker mid-scan, restart, assert scan completes. Auth test: try to access another user's file by id, assert 404. Pre-scan access test: upload large file, immediately try to GET, assert 409 NOT_READY.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would proxy upload through Node 'for security', then run scan inline before responding - 500MB request times out, Node memory spikes. They'd serve files immediately upon upload without scan gating, briefly exposing infected files. They'd not delete the S3 object on infection, leaving malware in storage.
